sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Mpox is a viral zoonotic disease caused by Orthopoxvirus (Poxviridae family), related to variola (smallpox) and vaccinia viruses. It is a notifiable disease in Australia. Mine site dormitory environments create elevated risk for transmission if a case is not rapidly identified and isolated.

**Incubation period:** 7–14 days (range 5–21 days)

**Infectious period:** From onset of prodrome until all lesions have scabbed and scabs have fully separated.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Prodromal Phase (days 1–5)**
- Fever, chills, headache, myalgia, arthralgia, back pain
- Fatigue
- **Lymphadenopathy** (distinguishes mpox from smallpox and chickenpox)

**Rash (begins 1–3 days after fever)**
- Classically: **face → palms/soles → genitalia → eyes**
- Lesion distribution: face (95%), palms/soles (75%), mouth (70%), genitalia (30%), eyes (20%)
- Progression: **macule → papule → vesicle → pustule → scab** (all lesions synchronous)
- Duration: 2–3 weeks

**Atypical Presentations**
- Mild or absent prodrome
- Minimal or single lesion
- Proctitis without rash

**Complications**
Cellulitis, pneumonia, sepsis, encephalitis, corneal infection/blindness

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**Transmission Routes**
- **Contact:** Skin-to-skin or contact with lesions, body fluids, contaminated materials (bedding, clothing, towels)
- **Droplet/Airborne:** Prolonged face-to-face contact; aerosol-generating procedures (AGPs) carry elevated risk

**Mine Site Context**
- Communal accommodation (dongas, camp dining) significantly increases outbreak potential
- Notify site medical officer and line management immediately if mpox suspected
- Affected worker must be isolated in private accommodation pending transfer
- Shared amenities (shower, gym, kitchen) that contact used before diagnosis must be identified for decontamination

**Differential Diagnosis**
- Chickenpox (varicella): lesions in different stages simultaneously; no lymphadenopathy
- Syphilis, herpes, molluscum contagiosum
- Impetigo, scabies

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
**Apply PPE before patient contact:**
- P2/N95 respirator (fit-tested)
- Gloves (double-glove for AGPs)
- Eye protection (goggles or face shield)
- Fluid-resistant gown or coveralls
- Apply surgical mask to patient if tolerated

**Clinical management:**
- Primary survey; manage complications (sepsis, respiratory distress, altered consciousness)
- Administer antipyretics/analgesia as indicated
- Isolate from other patients - **do not share transport with another patient**
- Notify receiving facility before arrival so isolation room can be prepared

**Wound care:**
- Do not puncture or drain lesions
- Cover open lesions with clean dry dressings

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- IV access and fluid resuscitation if sepsis or dehydration present
- Manage complications per relevant guidelines (Sepsis, Encephalitis, Pneumonia)
- If airway compromise: standard airway management with full PPE; aerosol precautions for all AGPs

**Vehicle Decontamination**
- Set vehicle airflow to directed exhaust (as per airflow protocol)
- Do not shake or agitate soiled linen - bag and label as clinical/infectious waste
- Vehicle cleaning per transmission-based precautions protocol
- Document case and notify infection control

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- **Mpox is a notifiable disease** - report to WA Department of Health within 24 hours
- Vaccination (modified vaccinia Ankara - Imvanex) available for high-risk contacts and outbreak response
- Contacts who shared accommodation or close contact during infectious period should be identified for public health follow-up
- Consider informing site management to facilitate environmental cleaning and contact tracing of shared spaces
